4887d456-c108-4405-bf91-19d5eefee99b:mpk.lastUserMessage:
Build me a WhatsApp community assistant bot.

The bot should work inside WhatsApp private chats and group chats. It should introduce itself when users say hi, start, or menu. It should have a main menu, help command, settings command, and about command.

The bot should understand simple text commands like:
menu
help
settings
about
rules
announcement
contact admin

When used in a group, the bot should know it is inside a group and explain that group admin features are available soon. It should help users understand the community rules, answer basic questions, and guide users back to the menu when they are confused.

The bot should not depend on slash commands because WhatsApp users may not use them. It should use simple numbered menus and short clear WhatsApp-style replies.

The bot should be friendly, professional, and easy to test.

.env.sample.content:
# Server port. Defaults to 3000 when missing.
PORT=3000

# Required for CookMyBots managed WhatsApp webhook verification.
# CookMyBots injects this for deployed bots. Do not share it publicly.
CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET=

- Files: .cookmybots/manifest.json, .env.sample, DOCS.md, README.md, package.json, src/bot.js, src/brain.js, src/index.js, src/lib/ai.js, src/lib/botProfile.js, src/lib/commands.js, src/lib/config.js, src/lib/errors.js, src/lib/format.js (+9 more)

DOCS.md.content:
# WhatsApp Community Assistant Bot

This project is a WhatsApp-only community assistant brain service for CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport.

CookMyBots handles the WhatsApp connection, phone pairing, session management, inbound message forwarding, and reply delivery. This service only receives managed webhook events and returns reply text.

It does not implement WhatsApp Cloud API webhooks and does not require WHATSAPP_TOKEN, WHATSAPP_PHONE_NUMBER_ID, WHATSAPP_VERIFY_TOKEN, Telegram, X, Discord, or any other platform code.

## What the bot does

The bot helps community members find basic community information in private chats and group chats.

It can show:

1) Rules
2) Announcements
3) Contact Admin guidance
4) Settings
5) About
6) Help

Private chats get a short introduction plus the menu. Group chats get shorter replies and a note that group admin features are coming soon.

No AI, external LLM, database, queue, or background worker is used in this version.

## HTTP endpoints

### GET /health

Returns service health and platform metadata.

### POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp

CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport sends inbound messages here.

The request must include:

Header:

X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret: your deployed CookMyBots webhook secret

Body fields accepted safely:

from, text, messageId, projectId, platform, source, timestamp, chatId, senderId, senderName, pushName, isGroup, chatType, groupId, metadata

The endpoint returns:

{
  "ok": true,
  "reply": "..."
}

### POST /test

Local test endpoint that does not require the webhook secret.

## Environment variables

PORT

Server port. Optional. Defaults to 3000 when missing.

CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET

Required for the managed WhatsApp webhook endpoint. CookMyBots injects this for deployed bots. It is used only to verify the X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret request header.

No WhatsApp Cloud API variables are required.

## Setup

Install dependencies:

npm install

Create a local environment file:

cp .env.sample .env

For local webhook testing, set CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET in .env. The /test endpoint can be used without it.

Run in development:

npm run dev

Run production start:

npm start

Build check:

npm run build

## Supported commands

The bot uses plain text commands, not slash commands.

hi, hello, hey, start, menu

Shows the main menu. In private chat, the bot introduces itself and explains what it can help with. In group chat, it keeps the reply concise and mentions that group admin features are coming soon.

rules or 1

Shows a concise default community rules list: respectful behavior, no spam, stay on topic, protect privacy, and contact admins for issues.

announcement, announcements, or 2

Shows: No active announcements yet. Admins can update this in a future version.

contact admin, admin, or 3

Explains how members can reach an admin using the built-in placeholder guidance.

settings or 4

Shows current preferences: language English, short reply style, and chat type. Editable settings are coming soon.

about or 5

Describes the assistant as a WhatsApp community helper for navigation, rules, announcements, and support.

help or 6

Lists available plain-text commands and explains numbered menu navigation.

Unknown messages

The bot replies with a friendly fallback and suggests typing menu or choosing a number. If the message appears to ask about community basics, it guides the user to rules, help, or contact admin without pretending to know unavailable community-specific details.

## Private chat test examples

Start the server:

npm run dev

Test menu:

curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/test \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"from":"user@s.whatsapp.net","text":"hi","messageId":"local-1"}'

Expected style:

Hi. I am your WhatsApp community assistant.
I help members find rules, announcements, admin contact guidance, settings, and help.

1) Rules
2) Announcements
3) Contact Admin
4) Settings
5) About
6) Help

Test rules:

curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/test \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"from":"user@s.whatsapp.net","text":"1","messageId":"local-2"}'

## Group-style test examples

curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/test \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"from":"group@g.us","chatId":"group@g.us","senderId":"user@s.whatsapp.net","text":"menu","messageId":"group-1","isGroup":true}'

Expected style:

I can help this group with community basics.
Group admin features are coming soon.

1) Rules
2) Announcements
3) Contact Admin
4) Settings
5) About
6) Help

Test group admin guidance:

curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/test \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"chatId":"group@g.us","senderId":"user@s.whatsapp.net","text":"contact admin","messageId":"group-2","isGroup":true}'

## Deployment notes

Deploy as a single Node.js service.

Set these environment variables in the hosting platform:

1) PORT if your host does not inject it automatically
2) CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET from CookMyBots

In CookMyBots, connect your WhatsApp number using managed transport. The bot service must expose:

POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp

## Logging and diagnostics

Startup logs include only booleans such as whether PORT and the webhook secret are set. The bot logs inbound handling attempts with non-sensitive metadata: platform, source, whether the chat is a group, whether a message id exists, and the matched command.

Errors use safe extraction:

err?.response?.data?.error?.message || err?.response?.data?.message || err?.message || String(err)

No secrets are logged.

README.md.content:
# WhatsApp Community Assistant Bot

A WhatsApp-only CookMyBots managed-transport brain service for community help menus, rules, announcements, admin contact guidance, settings, about, and help.

CookMyBots manages the WhatsApp connection. This project only exposes the bot brain endpoint:

POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp

## Features

1) Private and group chat aware replies
2) Plain-text commands, no slash-command dependency
3) Numbered WhatsApp-style menu
4) Friendly fallback for unknown messages
5) Production-safe logs with no secrets
6) No AI, database, polling loop, or extra platform code

## Run locally

npm install
cp .env.sample .env
npm run dev

Test locally:

curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/test \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"text":"menu"}'

Group-style test:

curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/test \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"chatId":"group@g.us","senderId":"user@s.whatsapp.net","text":"menu","isGroup":true}'

## Environment variables

PORT defaults to 3000 when missing.

CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET is required for the CookMyBots webhook endpoint and is injected by CookMyBots for deployed bots.

No WHATSAPP_TOKEN, WHATSAPP_PHONE_NUMBER_ID, WHATSAPP_VERIFY_TOKEN, AI key, or MongoDB URI is used.

## Commands

hi, hello, hey, start, menu
rules or 1
announcement, announcements, or 2
contact admin, admin, or 3
settings or 4
about or 5
help or 6

See DOCS.md for full setup, payload examples, and expected replies.

commands.example: User sends /help → bot replies with commands.
User sends /start → bot replies with intro.

Build me a WhatsApp community assistant bot.

The bot should work inside WhatsApp private chats and group chats. It should introduce itself when users say hi, start, or menu. It should have a main menu, help command, settings command, and about command.

The bot should understand simple text commands like:
menu
help
settings
about
rules
announcement
contact admin

When used in a group, the bot should know it is inside a group and explain that group admin features are available soon. It should help users understand the community rules, answer basic questions, and guide users back to the menu when they are confused.

The bot should not depend on slash commands because WhatsApp users may not use them. It should use simple numbered menus and short clear WhatsApp-style replies.

The bot should be friendly, professional, and easy to test.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: WhatsApp Community Assistant Bot

Summary:
A production-ready WhatsApp community assistant bot that runs as a single Node.js ESM brain service and uses CookMyBots managed transport for WhatsApp connectivity. The bot supports private chats and group chats, recognizes natural simple commands such as menu, help, rules, announcement, settings, about, and contact admin, and responds with short WhatsApp-style numbered menus.

The bot is designed to be friendly, professional, easy to test, and safe for community use. It does not rely on slash commands, does not implement WhatsApp Cloud API webhooks, and does not require WhatsApp platform tokens inside the bot service. CookMyBots handles message delivery and WhatsApp transport; this service focuses only on conversation logic, optional persistence, configuration, and response generation.

Product goals:
1) Provide a simple community front desk inside WhatsApp.
The bot should help members quickly find rules, announcements, admin contact guidance, help, settings, and about information without needing to remember complex syntax.

2) Work naturally in both private chats and groups.
In private chats, the bot can provide a fuller menu and guide the user through options. In group chats, it should detect the group context, keep replies concise, avoid spamming, and explain that group admin features are planned for a future release.

3) Avoid slash-command dependency.
WhatsApp users often type plain language. The bot should accept commands like menu, help, rules, announcement, contact admin, hi, hello, start, and numbered menu selections such as 1, 2, 3, and 4.

4) Be easy to test and operate.
The first release should use deterministic command matching, clear fallback responses, and configurable community text. A tester should be able to send hi, menu, rules, announcement, help, settings, about, and contact admin in private and group chats and confirm expected behavior.

5) Be production-ready without being over-engineered.
The service should run as one Node.js process, use MongoDB when persistence is needed, and keep simple in-memory maps acceptable for local testing or very small deployments. No external queues, extra databases, or additional messaging infrastructure are required.

High-level architecture:
The bot is the brain service only. CookMyBots handles WhatsApp transport, message delivery, and platform connectivity. The Node.js service receives normalized inbound message events from CookMyBots and returns outbound response instructions through the CookMyBots integration path.

Core modules:

1) Transport adapter
Receives CookMyBots managed transport events and converts them into an internal message format. The internal format should include message id, chat id, sender id, sender display name if available, text body, timestamp, chat type, and group metadata if available.

2) Message router
Normalizes incoming text, detects intent, and routes to the correct handler. Normalization should trim whitespace, lowercase text for matching, collapse repeated spaces, and support common greetings and synonyms.

3) Context detector
Determines whether the message is from a private chat or a group chat. Group replies should be shorter and should avoid too much back-and-forth. Private replies can include fuller menus and settings guidance.

4) Command and intent handlers
Each supported command has a focused handler: menu, help, settings, about, rules, announcement, and contact admin. Handlers produce short, structured WhatsApp-style replies with numbered options.

5) Menu state manager
Tracks the last menu shown to each user or chat so that numeric replies like 1, 2, 3, and 4 can map to the right action. For production, store this in MongoDB with a short expiration window. For local testing, an in-memory map is sufficient.

6) Community content provider
Stores configurable text for community name, rules, announcements, admin contact instructions, about text, help text, and feature flags. In production, this can come from MongoDB. For a first version, these values can also be loaded from environment configuration or a simple local configuration file.

7) Response composer
Formats messages in a consistent tone: friendly, professional, concise, and mobile-friendly. It should avoid long paragraphs in WhatsApp, use numbered menus, and always provide a path back to menu or help.

8) Safety and fallback layer
Handles unknown input, confused users, repeated messages, and unsupported features. For example, in groups the bot can say that group admin tools are coming soon and suggest typing rules, announcement, or contact admin.

9) Observability layer
Logs inbound events, detected intent, response outcome, and errors without storing sensitive message content unnecessarily. Production logs should help diagnose failures while respecting user privacy.

Data model:
MongoDB is recommended for production persistence. In-memory maps are acceptable for development, automated testing, or very small pilots where data loss on restart is acceptable.

Suggested MongoDB collections:

1) communityconfig
Stores the active community profile and editable content.
Fields: communityId, communityName, welcomeText, rulesText, announcementText, adminContactText, aboutText, helpText, settingsOptions, featureFlags, updatedAt.

2) userprofiles
Stores lightweight user preferences and metadata.
Fields: userId, displayName, preferredLanguage, mutedBotHints, firstSeenAt, lastSeenAt, lastPrivateChatAt, settings, updatedAt.

3) chatprofiles
Stores chat-level metadata.
Fields: chatId, chatType, groupName, firstSeenAt, lastSeenAt, groupFeatureStatus, updatedAt.

4) conversationstate
Stores temporary menu state.
Fields: scopeKey, userId, chatId, chatType, lastMenu, validOptions, expiresAt, updatedAt. The scopeKey should distinguish private user state from group-specific user state so that a numeric reply in a group does not accidentally trigger an unrelated private menu.

5) admincontactrequests
Stores contact-admin submissions or intent records.
Fields: requestId, userId, chatId, chatType, displayName, messageSummary, status, createdAt, updatedAt. In the first release, this can simply record that a user requested admin contact and return configured admin instructions.

6) messagededupe
Stores recently processed message ids to avoid duplicate replies if CookMyBots retries an event.
Fields: messageId, chatId, processedAt, expiresAt.

Important data principles:

1) Store only what the bot needs.
Do not keep full message history unless there is a clear product requirement.

2) Keep community text configurable.
Rules, announcements, and admin contact instructions should not require code changes.

3) Use expiration for temporary state.
Conversation menu state and dedupe records should expire automatically after a practical window.

4) Separate user state from chat state.
A person can interact with the bot privately and inside multiple groups. The model should avoid mixing these contexts.

Key flows:
1) Greeting flow
User sends hi, hello, hey, start, or menu. The bot detects a greeting or menu request and replies with an introduction and numbered menu.

Private chat example behavior: welcome the user, explain that the bot can help with community information, and show options such as 1) Rules, 2) Latest announcement, 3) Contact admin, 4) Help, 5) Settings, 6) About.

Group chat example behavior: greet briefly, say it can help with community information, show a shorter menu, and mention that group admin features are coming soon.

2) Main menu flow
User sends menu or a recognized greeting. The bot returns the main menu and stores valid numeric options in conversationstate. If the user replies 1, the router maps that selection to rules; 2 maps to announcement; 3 maps to contact admin; and so on.

3) Help flow
User sends help or says they are confused. The bot explains the available commands in plain language and suggests typing menu at any time. In groups, the help reply should be concise to avoid clutter.

4) Settings flow
User sends settings. The bot shows available settings, such as language preference or notification preference, if supported. If settings are not fully implemented yet, the bot should be transparent and say basic settings are available soon while still offering menu, help, rules, and about.

5) About flow
User sends about. The bot describes what it does, who it is for, and how to get back to the menu. It should also mention that WhatsApp delivery is handled through CookMyBots managed transport while the community bot service handles the assistant logic.

6) Rules flow
User sends rules or selects the rules menu option. The bot returns the configured community rules in a short numbered format. If the rules are long, show the most important rules first and offer to continue or contact admin.

7) Announcement flow
User sends announcement, announcements, latest, or news. The bot returns the current configured announcement. If there is no active announcement, it should say there are no new announcements and suggest menu.

8) Contact admin flow
User sends contact admin, admin, moderator, or support. The bot returns configured admin contact instructions. Optionally, it can create an admincontactrequests record for tracking. In a later release, this can become a more complete ticket-style flow.

9) Group context flow
When the message is from a group, the bot should identify the context from CookMyBots metadata and adjust behavior. It should not pretend to moderate the group if those features are not implemented. It can say: I can help with rules, announcements, and admin contact. Group admin tools are coming soon.

10) Unknown input flow
If the bot cannot understand a message, it should respond with a friendly fallback. The fallback should be short and should guide the user back to the menu. Example behavior: I did not catch that. Type menu to see options, or try rules, announcement, help, or contact admin.

11) Duplicate event flow
Before responding, the bot checks whether the incoming message id was already processed. If yes, it does not send a second reply. This prevents duplicate responses on event retries.

12) Error flow
If a handler fails, the bot logs the error and returns a safe generic message where appropriate: Sorry, I had trouble with that. Please try menu again. The service should avoid exposing internal details to users.

Tech stack:
Runtime and language:

1) Single Node.js service/process using ESM.
2) The service contains the bot logic, command routing, content lookup, state management, and response composition.
3) No separate worker service is required for the first production version.

WhatsApp transport:

1) WhatsApp connectivity is handled by CookMyBots managed transport.
2) The bot service must not implement WhatsApp Cloud API webhooks.
3) The bot service must not require WHATSAPPTOKEN, WHATSAPPPHONENUMBERID, or WHATSAPPVERIFYTOKEN.
4) The bot should consume normalized CookMyBots message events and send replies through the CookMyBots-managed integration contract.

Persistence:

1) MongoDB is the recommended production persistence option because it is already integrated.
2) Simple in-memory maps are acceptable for local testing, demos, or optional non-critical state.
3) Do not introduce PostgreSQL, MySQL, Redis, Kafka, RabbitMQ, or any other database or queue.

Storage:

1) The first release is text-first and does not require file or image storage.
2) If future community assets are needed, use object storage or simple filesystem storage in generic terms only.

Deployment:

1) Run as a Node.js server in the cloud or another managed server environment.
2) Keep configuration environment-based for community id, bot name, default language, MongoDB connection settings, and feature flags.
3) Do not require additional infrastructure beyond the single Node.js service, CookMyBots transport, and optional MongoDB.

Testing approach:

1) Unit test text normalization, intent detection, menu mapping, group/private context behavior, and fallback replies.
2) Integration test the CookMyBots adapter with sample inbound event payloads.
3) Manually test private chat and group chat scenarios using real WhatsApp conversations through CookMyBots.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability:

1) Use idempotency based on message id to prevent duplicate replies.
2) Keep all command handlers deterministic and fast.
3) Return a safe fallback if community content is missing.
4) Store temporary menu state with expiration so stale numeric replies do not trigger surprising actions.

Performance:

1) The bot should respond quickly because it uses simple command matching and lightweight database lookups.
2) Cache community configuration in memory with periodic refresh or reload on update to reduce repeated database reads.
3) Avoid long-running operations inside message handling.

Security and privacy:

1) Store minimal user data.
2) Avoid logging sensitive message content unless needed for debugging and explicitly allowed.
3) Do not expose internal errors, tokens, connection strings, or operational details to users.
4) Treat group messages carefully because replies are visible to multiple people.

Maintainability:

1) Keep each command handler small and isolated.
2) Keep content configurable so community managers can update rules and announcements without changing command logic.
3) Keep the intent matcher simple for version one, then add more natural-language handling only when needed.
4) Separate private-chat behavior from group-chat behavior in a clear policy layer.

User experience:

1) Replies should be short, clear, and mobile-friendly.
2) Always offer a next step, usually type menu.
3) Use friendly professional language.
4) Do not over-reply in groups.
5) Make unimplemented features explicit instead of pretending they exist.

Operational readiness:

1) Track basic metrics through logs: inbound messages, matched intents, unknown inputs, errors, and response outcomes.
2) Provide configuration validation on startup.
3) Include a simple health check endpoint or equivalent process-level health signal if supported by the hosting environment.
4) Have a rollback-friendly release process by keeping community content separate from bot logic.

Bot commands:
• hi — Starts the conversation with a friendly introduction. Also covers hello, hey, start, and similar greetings. (usage: Send hi, hello, hey, start, or menu) [onboarding]
• menu — Shows the main numbered menu. In private chat it shows the full menu. In group chat it shows a shorter group-safe menu and explains that group admin features are coming soon. (usage: menu) [navigation]
• help — Explains what the bot can do and lists the main text commands users can type without using slash commands. (usage: help) [support]
• settings — Shows user settings or explains which settings are available soon. It should always offer a way back to the menu. (usage: settings) [account]
• about — Explains the purpose of the community assistant, what it can help with, and how users can continue. (usage: about) [information]
• rules — Returns the configured community rules in a clear numbered format suitable for WhatsApp. (usage: rules) [community]
• announcement — Shows the latest configured community announcement or says there are no active announcements. (usage: announcement) [community]
• contact admin — Gives users the approved way to contact an admin or moderator. Optionally records the request for admin follow-up. (usage: contact admin) [support]
• numbered menu selection — Allows users to reply with simple numbers after a menu is shown, such as 1 for rules or 2 for announcement. (usage: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, or 6 after the bot shows a menu) [navigation]

1) Provide a simple community front desk inside WhatsApp.
The bot should help members quickly find rules, announcements, admin contact guidance, help, settings, and about information without needing to remember complex syntax.

2) Work naturally in both private chats and groups.
In private chats, the bot can provide a fuller menu and guide the user through options. In group chats, it should detect the group context, keep replies concise, avoid spamming, and explain that group admin features are planned for a future release.

3) Avoid slash-command dependency.
WhatsApp users often type plain language. The bot should accept commands like menu, help, rules, announcement, contact admin, hi, hello, start, and numbered menu selections such as 1, 2, 3, and 4.

4) Be easy to test and operate.
The first release should use deterministic command matching, clear fallback responses, and configurable community text. A tester should be able to send hi, menu, rules, announcement, help, settings, about, and contact admin in private and group chats and confirm expected behavior.

5) Be production-ready without being over-engineered.
The service should run as one Node.js process, use MongoDB when persistence is needed, and keep simple in-memory maps acceptable for local testing or very small deployments. No external queues, extra databases, or additional messaging infrastructure are required.

1) Transport adapter

2) Message router

Tracks the last menu shown to each user or chat so that numeric replies like 1, 2, 3, and 4 can map to the right action. For production, store this in MongoDB with a short expiration window. For local testing, an in-memory map is sufficient.

6) Community content provider
Stores configurable text for community name, rules, announcements, admin contact instructions, about text, help text, and feature flags. In production, this can come from MongoDB. For a first version, these values can also be loaded from environment configuration or a simple local configuration file.

7) Response composer
Formats messages in a consistent tone: friendly, professional, concise, and mobile-friendly. It should avoid long paragraphs in WhatsApp, use numbered menus, and always provide a path back to menu or help.

8) Safety and fallback layer
Handles unknown input, confused users, repeated messages, and unsupported features. For example, in groups the bot can say that group admin tools are coming soon and suggest typing rules, announcement, or contact admin.

9) Observability layer
Logs inbound events, detected intent, response outcome, and errors without storing sensitive message content unnecessarily. Production logs should help diagnose failures while respecting user privacy.
planSections.dataModel:
MongoDB is recommended for production persistence. In-memory maps are acceptable for development, automated testing, or very small pilots where data loss on restart is acceptable.

Suggested MongoDB collections:

1) communityconfig
Stores the active community profile and editable content.
Fields: communityId, communityName, welcomeText, rulesText, announcementText, adminContactText, aboutText, helpText, settingsOptions, featureFlags, updatedAt.

2) userprofiles
Stores lightweight user preferences and metadata.
Fields: userId, displayName, preferredLanguage, mutedBotHints, firstSeenAt, lastSeenAt, lastPrivateChatAt, settings, updatedAt.

3) chatprofiles
Stores chat-level metadata.
Fields: chatId, chatType, groupName, firstSeenAt, lastSeenAt, groupFeatureStatus, updatedAt.

4) conversationstate
Stores temporary menu state.
Fields: scopeKey, userId, chatId, chatType, lastMenu, validOptions, expiresAt, updatedAt. The scopeKey should distinguish private user state from group-specific user state so that a numeric reply in a group does not accidentally trigger an unrelated private menu.

5) admincontactrequests
Stores contact-admin submissions or intent records.
Fields: requestId, userId, chatId, chatType, displayName, messageSummary, status, createdAt, updatedAt. In the first release, this can simply record that a user requested admin contact and return configured admin instructions.

6) messagededupe
Stores recently processed message ids to avoid duplicate replies if CookMyBots retries an event.
Fields: messageId, chatId, processedAt, expiresAt.

Important data principles:

1) Store only what the bot needs.
Do not keep full message history unless there is a clear product requirement.

2) Keep community text configurable.
Rules, announcements, and admin contact instructions should not require code changes.

3) Use expiration for temporary state.
Conversation menu state and dedupe records should expire automatically after a practical window.

4) Separate user state from chat state.
A person can interact with the bot privately and inside multiple groups. The model should avoid mixing these contexts.
planSections.keyFlows:
1) Greeting flow
User sends hi, hello, hey, start, or menu. The bot detects a greeting or menu request and replies with an introduction and numbered menu.

Private chat example behavior: welcome the user, explain that the bot can help with community information, and show options such as 1) Rules, 2) Latest announcement, 3) Contact admin, 4) Help, 5) Settings, 6) About.

Group chat example behavior: greet briefly, say it can help with community information, show a shorter menu, and mention that group admin features are coming soon.

2) Main menu flow
User sends menu or a recognized greeting. The bot returns the main menu and stores valid numeric options in conversationstate. If the user replies 1, the router maps that selection to rules; 2 maps to announcement; 3 maps to contact admin; and so on.

3) Help flow
User sends help or says they are confused. The bot explains the available commands in plain language and suggests typing menu at any time. In groups, the help reply should be concise to avoid clutter.

4) Settings flow
User sends settings. The bot shows available settings, such as language preference or notification preference, if supported. If settings are not fully implemented yet, the bot should be transparent and say basic settings are available soon while still offering menu, help, rules, and about.

5) About flow
User sends about. The bot describes what it does, who it is for, and how to get back to the menu. It should also mention that WhatsApp delivery is handled through CookMyBots managed transport while the community bot service handles the assistant logic.

6) Rules flow
User sends rules or selects the rules menu option. The bot returns the configured community rules in a short numbered format. If the rules are long, show the most important rules first and offer to continue or contact admin.

7) Announcement flow
User sends announcement, announcements, latest, or news. The bot returns the current configured announcement. If there is no active announcement, it should say there are no new announcements and suggest menu.

8) Contact admin flow
User sends contact admin, admin, moderator, or support. The bot returns configured admin contact instructions. Optionally, it can create an admincontactrequests record for tracking. In a later release, this can become a more complete ticket-style flow.

9) Group context flow
When the message is from a group, the bot should identify the context from CookMyBots metadata and adjust behavior. It should not pretend to moderate the group if those features are not implemented. It can say: I can help with rules, announcements, and admin contact. Group admin tools are coming soon.

10) Unknown input flow
If the bot cannot understand a message, it should respond with a friendly fallback. The fallback should be short and should guide the user back to the menu. Example behavior: I did not catch that. Type menu to see options, or try rules, announcement, help, or contact admin.

11) Duplicate event flow
Before responding, the bot checks whether the incoming message id was already processed. If yes, it does not send a second reply. This prevents duplicate responses on event retries.

12) Error flow
If a handler fails, the bot logs the error and returns a safe generic message where appropriate: Sorry, I had trouble with that. Please try menu again. The service should avoid exposing internal details to users.
planSections.techStack:
Runtime and language:

1) Single Node.js service/process using ESM.
2) The service contains the bot logic, command routing, content lookup, state management, and response composition.
3) No separate worker service is required for the first production version.

WhatsApp transport:

1) WhatsApp connectivity is handled by CookMyBots managed transport.
2) The bot service must not implement WhatsApp Cloud API webhooks.
3) The bot service must not require WHATSAPPTOKEN, WHATSAPPPHONENUMBERID, or WHATSAPPVERIFYTOKEN.
4) The bot should consume normalized CookMyBots message events and send replies through the CookMyBots-managed integration contract.

Persistence:

1) MongoDB is the recommended production persistence option because it is already integrated.
2) Simple in-memory maps are acceptable for local testing, demos, or optional non-critical state.
3) Do not introduce PostgreSQL, MySQL, Redis, Kafka, RabbitMQ, or any other database or queue.

Storage:

1) The first release is text-first and does not require file or image storage.
2) If future community assets are needed, use object storage or simple filesystem storage in generic terms only.

Deployment:

1) Run as a Node.js server in the cloud or another managed server environment.
2) Keep configuration environment-based for community id, bot name, default language, MongoDB connection settings, and feature flags.
3) Do not require additional infrastructure beyond the single Node.js service, CookMyBots transport, and optional MongoDB.

Testing approach:

1) Unit test text normalization, intent detection, menu mapping, group/private context behavior, and fallback replies.
2) Integration test the CookMyBots adapter with sample inbound event payloads.
3) Manually test private chat and group chat scenarios using real WhatsApp conversations through CookMyBots.
planSections.nonFunctional:
Reliability:

1) Use idempotency based on message id to prevent duplicate replies.
2) Keep all command handlers deterministic and fast.
3) Return a safe fallback if community content is missing.
4) Store temporary menu state with expiration so stale numeric replies do not trigger surprising actions.

Performance:

1) The bot should respond quickly because it uses simple command matching and lightweight database lookups.
2) Cache community configuration in memory with periodic refresh or reload on update to reduce repeated database reads.
3) Avoid long-running operations inside message handling.

Security and privacy:

1) Store minimal user data.
2) Avoid logging sensitive message content unless needed for debugging and explicitly allowed.
3) Do not expose internal errors, tokens, connection strings, or operational details to users.
4) Treat group messages carefully because replies are visible to multiple people.

Maintainability:

1) Keep each command handler small and isolated.
2) Keep content configurable so community managers can update rules and announcements without changing command logic.
3) Keep the intent matcher simple for version one, then add more natural-language handling only when needed.
4) Separate private-chat behavior from group-chat behavior in a clear policy layer.

User experience:

1) Replies should be short, clear, and mobile-friendly.
2) Always offer a next step, usually type menu.
3) Use friendly professional language.
4) Do not over-reply in groups.
5) Make unimplemented features explicit instead of pretending they exist.

Operational readiness:

1) Track basic metrics through logs: inbound messages, matched intents, unknown inputs, errors, and response outcomes.
2) Provide configuration validation on startup.
3) Include a simple health check endpoint or equivalent process-level health signal if supported by the hosting environment.
4) Have a rollback-friendly release process by keeping community content separate from bot logic.

- Files: .cookmybots/manifest.json, .env.sample, DOCS.md, README.md, package.json, src/bot.js, src/brain.js, src/index.js, src/lib/ai.js, src/lib/botProfile.js, src/lib/commands.js, src/lib/config.js, src/lib/errors.js, src/lib/format.js (+9 more)

DOCS.md.content:
# WhatsApp Community Assistant Bot

This project is a WhatsApp-only community assistant brain service for CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport.

CookMyBots handles the WhatsApp connection, phone pairing, session management, inbound message forwarding, and reply delivery. This service only receives managed webhook events and returns reply text.

It does not implement WhatsApp Cloud API webhooks and does not require WHATSAPP_TOKEN, WHATSAPP_PHONE_NUMBER_ID, WHATSAPP_VERIFY_TOKEN, Telegram, X, Discord, or any other platform code.

## What the bot does

The bot helps community members find basic community information in private chats and group chats.

It can show:

1) Rules
2) Announcements
3) Contact Admin guidance
4) Settings
5) About
6) Help

Private chats get a short introduction plus the menu. Group chats get shorter replies and a note that group admin features are coming soon.

No AI, external LLM, database, queue, or background worker is used in this version.

## HTTP endpoints

### GET /health

Returns service health and platform metadata.

### POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp

CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport sends inbound messages here.

The request must include:

Header:

X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret: your deployed CookMyBots webhook secret

Body fields accepted safely:

from, text, messageId, projectId, platform, source, timestamp, chatId, senderId, senderName, pushName, isGroup, chatType, groupId, metadata

The endpoint returns:

{
  "ok": true,
  "reply": "..."
}

### POST /test

Local test endpoint that does not require the webhook secret.

## Environment variables

PORT

Server port. Optional. Defaults to 3000 when missing.

CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET

Required for the managed WhatsApp webhook endpoint. CookMyBots injects this for deployed bots. It is used only to verify the X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret request header.

No WhatsApp Cloud API variables are required.

## Setup

Install dependencies:

npm install

Create a local environment file:

cp .env.sample .env

For local webhook testing, set CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET in .env. The /test endpoint can be used without it.

Run in development:

npm run dev

Run production start:

npm start

Build check:

npm run build

## Supported commands

The bot uses plain text commands, not slash commands.

hi, hello, hey, start, menu

Shows the main menu. In private chat, the bot introduces itself and explains what it can help with. In group chat, it keeps the reply concise and mentions that group admin features are coming soon.

rules or 1

Shows a concise default community rules list: respectful behavior, no spam, stay on topic, protect privacy, and contact admins for issues.

announcement, announcements, or 2

Shows: No active announcements yet. Admins can update this in a future version.

contact admin, admin, or 3

Explains how members can reach an admin using the built-in placeholder guidance.

settings or 4

Shows current preferences: language English, short reply style, and chat type. Editable settings are coming soon.

about or 5

Describes the assistant as a WhatsApp community helper for navigation, rules, announcements, and support.

help or 6

Lists available plain-text commands and explains numbered menu navigation.

Unknown messages

The bot replies with a friendly fallback and suggests typing menu or choosing a number. If the message appears to ask about community basics, it guides the user to rules, help, or contact admin without pretending to know unavailable community-specific details.

## Private chat test examples

Start the server:

npm run dev

Test menu:

curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/test \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"from":"user@s.whatsapp.net","text":"hi","messageId":"local-1"}'

Expected style:

Hi. I am your WhatsApp community assistant.
I help members find rules, announcements, admin contact guidance, settings, and help.

1) Rules
2) Announcements
3) Contact Admin
4) Settings
5) About
6) Help

Test rules:

curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/test \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"from":"user@s.whatsapp.net","text":"1","messageId":"local-2"}'

## Group-style test examples

curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/test \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"from":"group@g.us","chatId":"group@g.us","senderId":"user@s.whatsapp.net","text":"menu","messageId":"group-1","isGroup":true}'

Expected style:

I can help this group with community basics.
Group admin features are coming soon.

1) Rules
2) Announcements
3) Contact Admin
4) Settings
5) About
6) Help

Test group admin guidance:

curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/test \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"chatId":"group@g.us","senderId":"user@s.whatsapp.net","text":"contact admin","messageId":"group-2","isGroup":true}'

## Deployment notes

Deploy as a single Node.js service.

Set these environment variables in the hosting platform:

1) PORT if your host does not inject it automatically
2) CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET from CookMyBots

In CookMyBots, connect your WhatsApp number using managed transport. The bot service must expose:

POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp

## Logging and diagnostics

Startup logs include only booleans such as whether PORT and the webhook secret are set. The bot logs inbound handling attempts with non-sensitive metadata: platform, source, whether the chat is a group, whether a message id exists, and the matched command.

Errors use safe extraction:

err?.response?.data?.error?.message || err?.response?.data?.message || err?.message || String(err)

No secrets are logged.

README.md.content:
# WhatsApp Community Assistant Bot

A WhatsApp-only CookMyBots managed-transport brain service for community help menus, rules, announcements, admin contact guidance, settings, about, and help.

CookMyBots manages the WhatsApp connection. This project only exposes the bot brain endpoint:

POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp

## Features

1) Private and group chat aware replies
2) Plain-text commands, no slash-command dependency
3) Numbered WhatsApp-style menu
4) Friendly fallback for unknown messages
5) Production-safe logs with no secrets
6) No AI, database, polling loop, or extra platform code

## Run locally

npm install
cp .env.sample .env
npm run dev

Test locally:

curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/test \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"text":"menu"}'

Group-style test:

curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/test \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"chatId":"group@g.us","senderId":"user@s.whatsapp.net","text":"menu","isGroup":true}'

## Environment variables

PORT defaults to 3000 when missing.

CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET is required for the CookMyBots webhook endpoint and is injected by CookMyBots for deployed bots.

No WHATSAPP_TOKEN, WHATSAPP_PHONE_NUMBER_ID, WHATSAPP_VERIFY_TOKEN, AI key, or MongoDB URI is used.

## Commands

hi, hello, hey, start, menu
rules or 1
announcement, announcements, or 2
contact admin, admin, or 3
settings or 4
about or 5
help or 6

See DOCS.md for full setup, payload examples, and expected replies.

commands.example: User sends /help → bot replies with commands.
User sends /start → bot replies with intro.

commandDocs.notes: Private chats get a fuller introduction. Group chats get shorter replies.

commandDocs.description: Shows the numbered WhatsApp community menu.
examples: User: menu → Bot: 1) Rules 2) Announcements 3) Contact Admin 4) Settings 5) About 6) Help

commandDocs.description: Shows concise default community rules.
examples: User: 1 → Bot: community rules list

commandDocs.description: Shows the current announcement placeholder.
examples: User: announcement → Bot: No active announcements yet.

commandDocs.description: Shows guidance for reaching a community admin.
examples: User: 3 → Bot: admin contact guidance

examples: User: about → Bot: WhatsApp community helper description

examples: User: help → Bot: command list

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: WhatsApp Community Assistant Bot

Summary:
A production-ready WhatsApp community assistant bot that runs as a single Node.js ESM brain service and uses CookMyBots managed transport for WhatsApp connectivity. The bot supports private chats and group chats, recognizes natural simple commands such as menu, help, rules, announcement, settings, about, and contact admin, and responds with short WhatsApp-style numbered menus.

The bot is designed to be friendly, professional, easy to test, and safe for community use. It does not rely on slash commands, does not implement WhatsApp Cloud API webhooks, and does not require WhatsApp platform tokens inside the bot service. CookMyBots handles message delivery and WhatsApp transport; this service focuses only on conversation logic, optional persistence, configuration, and response generation.

Product goals:
1) Provide a simple community front desk inside WhatsApp.
The bot should help members quickly find rules, announcements, admin contact guidance, help, settings, and about information without needing to remember complex syntax.

2) Work naturally in both private chats and groups.
In private chats, the bot can provide a fuller menu and guide the user through options. In group chats, it should detect the group context, keep replies concise, avoid spamming, and explain that group admin features are planned for a future release.

3) Avoid slash-command dependency.
WhatsApp users often type plain language. The bot should accept commands like menu, help, rules, announcement, contact admin, hi, hello, start, and numbered menu selections such as 1, 2, 3, and 4.

4) Be easy to test and operate.
The first release should use deterministic command matching, clear fallback responses, and configurable community text. A tester should be able to send hi, menu, rules, announcement, help, settings, about, and contact admin in private and group chats and confirm expected behavior.

5) Be production-ready without being over-engineered.
The service should run as one Node.js process, use MongoDB when persistence is needed, and keep simple in-memory maps acceptable for local testing or very small deployments. No external queues, extra databases, or additional messaging infrastructure are required.

High-level architecture:
The bot is the brain service only. CookMyBots handles WhatsApp transport, message delivery, and platform connectivity. The Node.js service receives normalized inbound message events from CookMyBots and returns outbound response instructions through the CookMyBots integration path.

Core modules:

1) Transport adapter
Receives CookMyBots managed transport events and converts them into an internal message format. The internal format should include message id, chat id, sender id, sender display name if available, text body, timestamp, chat type, and group metadata if available.

2) Message router
Normalizes incoming text, detects intent, and routes to the correct handler. Normalization should trim whitespace, lowercase text for matching, collapse repeated spaces, and support common greetings and synonyms.

3) Context detector
Determines whether the message is from a private chat or a group chat. Group replies should be shorter and should avoid too much back-and-forth. Private replies can include fuller menus and settings guidance.

4) Command and intent handlers
Each supported command has a focused handler: menu, help, settings, about, rules, announcement, and contact admin. Handlers produce short, structured WhatsApp-style replies with numbered options.

5) Menu state manager
Tracks the last menu shown to each user or chat so that numeric replies like 1, 2, 3, and 4 can map to the right action. For production, store this in MongoDB with a short expiration window. For local testing, an in-memory map is sufficient.

6) Community content provider
Stores configurable text for community name, rules, announcements, admin contact instructions, about text, help text, and feature flags. In production, this can come from MongoDB. For a first version, these values can also be loaded from environment configuration or a simple local configuration file.

7) Response composer
Formats messages in a consistent tone: friendly, professional, concise, and mobile-friendly. It should avoid long paragraphs in WhatsApp, use numbered menus, and always provide a path back to menu or help.

8) Safety and fallback layer
Handles unknown input, confused users, repeated messages, and unsupported features. For example, in groups the bot can say that group admin tools are coming soon and suggest typing rules, announcement, or contact admin.

9) Observability layer
Logs inbound events, detected intent, response outcome, and errors without storing sensitive message content unnecessarily. Production logs should help diagnose failures while respecting user privacy.

Data model:
MongoDB is recommended for production persistence. In-memory maps are acceptable for development, automated testing, or very small pilots where data loss on restart is acceptable.

Suggested MongoDB collections:

1) communityconfig
Stores the active community profile and editable content.
Fields: communityId, communityName, welcomeText, rulesText, announcementText, adminContactText, aboutText, helpText, settingsOptions, featureFlags, updatedAt.

2) userprofiles
Stores lightweight user preferences and metadata.
Fields: userId, displayName, preferredLanguage, mutedBotHints, firstSeenAt, lastSeenAt, lastPrivateChatAt, settings, updatedAt.

3) chatprofiles
Stores chat-level metadata.
Fields: chatId, chatType, groupName, firstSeenAt, lastSeenAt, groupFeatureStatus, updatedAt.

4) conversationstate
Stores temporary menu state.
Fields: scopeKey, userId, chatId, chatType, lastMenu, validOptions, expiresAt, updatedAt. The scopeKey should distinguish private user state from group-specific user state so that a numeric reply in a group does not accidentally trigger an unrelated private menu.

5) admincontactrequests
Stores contact-admin submissions or intent records.
Fields: requestId, userId, chatId, chatType, displayName, messageSummary, status, createdAt, updatedAt. In the first release, this can simply record that a user requested admin contact and return configured admin instructions.

6) messagededupe
Stores recently processed message ids to avoid duplicate replies if CookMyBots retries an event.
Fields: messageId, chatId, processedAt, expiresAt.

Important data principles:

1) Store only what the bot needs.
Do not keep full message history unless there is a clear product requirement.

2) Keep community text configurable.
Rules, announcements, and admin contact instructions should not require code changes.

3) Use expiration for temporary state.
Conversation menu state and dedupe records should expire automatically after a practical window.

4) Separate user state from chat state.
A person can interact with the bot privately and inside multiple groups. The model should avoid mixing these contexts.

Key flows:
1) Greeting flow
User sends hi, hello, hey, start, or menu. The bot detects a greeting or menu request and replies with an introduction and numbered menu.

Private chat example behavior: welcome the user, explain that the bot can help with community information, and show options such as 1) Rules, 2) Latest announcement, 3) Contact admin, 4) Help, 5) Settings, 6) About.

Group chat example behavior: greet briefly, say it can help with community information, show a shorter menu, and mention that group admin features are coming soon.

2) Main menu flow
User sends menu or a recognized greeting. The bot returns the main menu and stores valid numeric options in conversationstate. If the user replies 1, the router maps that selection to rules; 2 maps to announcement; 3 maps to contact admin; and so on.

3) Help flow
User sends help or says they are confused. The bot explains the available commands in plain language and suggests typing menu at any time. In groups, the help reply should be concise to avoid clutter.

4) Settings flow
User sends settings. The bot shows available settings, such as language preference or notification preference, if supported. If settings are not fully implemented yet, the bot should be transparent and say basic settings are available soon while still offering menu, help, rules, and about.

5) About flow
User sends about. The bot describes what it does, who it is for, and how to get back to the menu. It should also mention that WhatsApp delivery is handled through CookMyBots managed transport while the community bot service handles the assistant logic.

6) Rules flow
User sends rules or selects the rules menu option. The bot returns the configured community rules in a short numbered format. If the rules are long, show the most important rules first and offer to continue or contact admin.

7) Announcement flow
User sends announcement, announcements, latest, or news. The bot returns the current configured announcement. If there is no active announcement, it should say there are no new announcements and suggest menu.

8) Contact admin flow
User sends contact admin, admin, moderator, or support. The bot returns configured admin contact instructions. Optionally, it can create an admincontactrequests record for tracking. In a later release, this can become a more complete ticket-style flow.

9) Group context flow
When the message is from a group, the bot should identify the context from CookMyBots metadata and adjust behavior. It should not pretend to moderate the group if those features are not implemented. It can say: I can help with rules, announcements, and admin contact. Group admin tools are coming soon.

10) Unknown input flow
If the bot cannot understand a message, it should respond with a friendly fallback. The fallback should be short and should guide the user back to the menu. Example behavior: I did not catch that. Type menu to see options, or try rules, announcement, help, or contact admin.

11) Duplicate event flow
Before responding, the bot checks whether the incoming message id was already processed. If yes, it does not send a second reply. This prevents duplicate responses on event retries.

12) Error flow
If a handler fails, the bot logs the error and returns a safe generic message where appropriate: Sorry, I had trouble with that. Please try menu again. The service should avoid exposing internal details to users.

Tech stack:
Runtime and language:

1) Single Node.js service/process using ESM.
2) The service contains the bot logic, command routing, content lookup, state management, and response composition.
3) No separate worker service is required for the first production version.

WhatsApp transport:

1) WhatsApp connectivity is handled by CookMyBots managed transport.
2) The bot service must not implement WhatsApp Cloud API webhooks.
3) The bot service must not require WHATSAPPTOKEN, WHATSAPPPHONENUMBERID, or WHATSAPPVERIFYTOKEN.
4) The bot should consume normalized CookMyBots message events and send replies through the CookMyBots-managed integration contract.

Persistence:

1) MongoDB is the recommended production persistence option because it is already integrated.
2) Simple in-memory maps are acceptable for local testing, demos, or optional non-critical state.
3) Do not introduce PostgreSQL, MySQL, Redis, Kafka, RabbitMQ, or any other database or queue.

Storage:

1) The first release is text-first and does not require file or image storage.
2) If future community assets are needed, use object storage or simple filesystem storage in generic terms only.

Deployment:

1) Run as a Node.js server in the cloud or another managed server environment.
2) Keep configuration environment-based for community id, bot name, default language, MongoDB connection settings, and feature flags.
3) Do not require additional infrastructure beyond the single Node.js service, CookMyBots transport, and optional MongoDB.

Testing approach:

1) Unit test text normalization, intent detection, menu mapping, group/private context behavior, and fallback replies.
2) Integration test the CookMyBots adapter with sample inbound event payloads.
3) Manually test private chat and group chat scenarios using real WhatsApp conversations through CookMyBots.

Non-functional requirements:
Reliability:

1) Use idempotency based on message id to prevent duplicate replies.
2) Keep all command handlers deterministic and fast.
3) Return a safe fallback if community content is missing.
4) Store temporary menu state with expiration so stale numeric replies do not trigger surprising actions.

Performance:

1) The bot should respond quickly because it uses simple command matching and lightweight database lookups.
2) Cache community configuration in memory with periodic refresh or reload on update to reduce repeated database reads.
3) Avoid long-running operations inside message handling.

Security and privacy:

1) Store minimal user data.
2) Avoid logging sensitive message content unless needed for debugging and explicitly allowed.
3) Do not expose internal errors, tokens, connection strings, or operational details to users.
4) Treat group messages carefully because replies are visible to multiple people.

Maintainability:

1) Keep each command handler small and isolated.
2) Keep content configurable so community managers can update rules and announcements without changing command logic.
3) Keep the intent matcher simple for version one, then add more natural-language handling only when needed.
4) Separate private-chat behavior from group-chat behavior in a clear policy layer.

User experience:

1) Replies should be short, clear, and mobile-friendly.
2) Always offer a next step, usually type menu.
3) Use friendly professional language.
4) Do not over-reply in groups.
5) Make unimplemented features explicit instead of pretending they exist.

Operational readiness:

1) Track basic metrics through logs: inbound messages, matched intents, unknown inputs, errors, and response outcomes.
2) Provide configuration validation on startup.
3) Include a simple health check endpoint or equivalent process-level health signal if supported by the hosting environment.
4) Have a rollback-friendly release process by keeping community content separate from bot logic.

Bot commands:
• hi — Starts the conversation with a friendly introduction. Also covers hello, hey, start, and similar greetings. (usage: Send hi, hello, hey, start, or menu) [onboarding]
• menu — Shows the main numbered menu. In private chat it shows the full menu. In group chat it shows a shorter group-safe menu and explains that group admin features are coming soon. (usage: menu) [navigation]
• help — Explains what the bot can do and lists the main text commands users can type without using slash commands. (usage: help) [support]
• settings — Shows user settings or explains which settings are available soon. It should always offer a way back to the menu. (usage: settings) [account]
• about — Explains the purpose of the community assistant, what it can help with, and how users can continue. (usage: about) [information]
• rules — Returns the configured community rules in a clear numbered format suitable for WhatsApp. (usage: rules) [community]
• announcement — Shows the latest configured community announcement or says there are no active announcements. (usage: announcement) [community]
• contact admin — Gives users the approved way to contact an admin or moderator. Optionally records the request for admin follow-up. (usage: contact admin) [support]
• numbered menu selection — Allows users to reply with simple numbers after a menu is shown, such as 1 for rules or 2 for announcement. (usage: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, or 6 after the bot shows a menu) [navigation]

planSections.productGoals:
1) Provide a simple community front desk inside WhatsApp.
The bot should help members quickly find rules, announcements, admin contact guidance, help, settings, and about information without needing to remember complex syntax.

2) Work naturally in both private chats and groups.
In private chats, the bot can provide a fuller menu and guide the user through options. In group chats, it should detect the group context, keep replies concise, avoid spamming, and explain that group admin features are planned for a future release.

3) Avoid slash-command dependency.
WhatsApp users often type plain language. The bot should accept commands like menu, help, rules, announcement, contact admin, hi, hello, start, and numbered menu selections such as 1, 2, 3, and 4.

4) Be easy to test and operate.
The first release should use deterministic command matching, clear fallback responses, and configurable community text. A tester should be able to send hi, menu, rules, announcement, help, settings, about, and contact admin in private and group chats and confirm expected behavior.

5) Be production-ready without being over-engineered.
The service should run as one Node.js process, use MongoDB when persistence is needed, and keep simple in-memory maps acceptable for local testing or very small deployments. No external queues, extra databases, or additional messaging infrastructure are required.
planSections.highLevelArchitecture:
The bot is the brain service only. CookMyBots handles WhatsApp transport, message delivery, and platform connectivity. The Node.js service receives normalized inbound message events from CookMyBots and returns outbound response instructions through the CookMyBots integration path.

Core modules:

1) Transport adapter
Receives CookMyBots managed transport events and converts them into an internal message format. The internal format should include message id, chat id, sender id, sender display name if available, text body, timestamp, chat type, and group metadata if available.

2) Message router
Normalizes incoming text, detects intent, and routes to the correct handler. Normalization should trim whitespace, lowercase text for matching, collapse repeated spaces, and support common greetings and synonyms.

3) Context detector
Determines whether the message is from a private chat or a group chat. Group replies should be shorter and should avoid too much back-and-forth. Private replies can include fuller menus and settings guidance.

4) Command and intent handlers
Each supported command has a focused handler: menu, help, settings, about, rules, announcement, and contact admin. Handlers produce short, structured WhatsApp-style replies with numbered options.

5) Menu state manager
Tracks the last menu shown to each user or chat so that numeric replies like 1, 2, 3, and 4 can map to the right action. For production, store this in MongoDB with a short expiration window. For local testing, an in-memory map is sufficient.

6) Community content provider
Stores configurable text for community name, rules, announcements, admin contact instructions, about text, help text, and feature flags. In production, this can come from MongoDB. For a first version, these values can also be loaded from environment configuration or a simple local configuration file.

7) Response composer
Formats messages in a consistent tone: friendly, professional, concise, and mobile-friendly. It should avoid long paragraphs in WhatsApp, use numbered menus, and always provide a path back to menu or help.

8) Safety and fallback layer
Handles unknown input, confused users, repeated messages, and unsupported features. For example, in groups the bot can say that group admin tools are coming soon and suggest typing rules, announcement, or contact admin.

9) Observability layer
Logs inbound events, detected intent, response outcome, and errors without storing sensitive message content unnecessarily. Production logs should help diagnose failures while respecting user privacy.
planSections.dataModel:
MongoDB is recommended for production persistence. In-memory maps are acceptable for development, automated testing, or very small pilots where data loss on restart is acceptable.

Suggested MongoDB collections:

1) communityconfig
Stores the active community profile and editable content.
Fields: communityId, communityName, welcomeText, rulesText, announcementText, adminContactText, aboutText, helpText, settingsOptions, featureFlags, updatedAt.

2) userprofiles
Stores lightweight user preferences and metadata.
Fields: userId, displayName, preferredLanguage, mutedBotHints, firstSeenAt, lastSeenAt, lastPrivateChatAt, settings, updatedAt.

3) chatprofiles
Stores chat-level metadata.
Fields: chatId, chatType, groupName, firstSeenAt, lastSeenAt, groupFeatureStatus, updatedAt.

4) conversationstate
Stores temporary menu state.
Fields: scopeKey, userId, chatId, chatType, lastMenu, validOptions, expiresAt, updatedAt. The scopeKey should distinguish private user state from group-specific user state so that a numeric reply in a group does not accidentally trigger an unrelated private menu.

5) admincontactrequests
Stores contact-admin submissions or intent records.
Fields: requestId, userId, chatId, chatType, displayName, messageSummary, status, createdAt, updatedAt. In the first release, this can simply record that a user requested admin contact and return configured admin instructions.

6) messagededupe
Stores recently processed message ids to avoid duplicate replies if CookMyBots retries an event.
Fields: messageId, chatId, processedAt, expiresAt.

Important data principles:

1) Store only what the bot needs.
Do not keep full message history unless there is a clear product requirement.

2) Keep community text configurable.
Rules, announcements, and admin contact instructions should not require code changes.

3) Use expiration for temporary state.
Conversation menu state and dedupe records should expire automatically after a practical window.

4) Separate user state from chat state.
A person can interact with the bot privately and inside multiple groups. The model should avoid mixing these contexts.
planSections.keyFlows:
1) Greeting flow
User sends hi, hello, hey, start, or menu. The bot detects a greeting or menu request and replies with an introduction and numbered menu.

Private chat example behavior: welcome the user, explain that the bot can help with community information, and show options such as 1) Rules, 2) Latest announcement, 3) Contact admin, 4) Help, 5) Settings, 6) About.

Group chat example behavior: greet briefly, say it can help with community information, show a shorter menu, and mention that group admin features are coming soon.

2) Main menu flow
User sends menu or a recognized greeting. The bot returns the main menu and stores valid numeric options in conversationstate. If the user replies 1, the router maps that selection to rules; 2 maps to announcement; 3 maps to contact admin; and so on.

3) Help flow
User sends help or says they are confused. The bot explains the available commands in plain language and suggests typing menu at any time. In groups, the help reply should be concise to avoid clutter.

4) Settings flow
User sends settings. The bot shows available settings, such as language preference or notification preference, if supported. If settings are not fully implemented yet, the bot should be transparent and say basic settings are available soon while still offering menu, help, rules, and about.

5) About flow
User sends about. The bot describes what it does, who it is for, and how to get back to the menu. It should also mention that WhatsApp delivery is handled through CookMyBots managed transport while the community bot service handles the assistant logic.

6) Rules flow
User sends rules or selects the rules menu option. The bot returns the configured community rules in a short numbered format. If the rules are long, show the most important rules first and offer to continue or contact admin.

7) Announcement flow
User sends announcement, announcements, latest, or news. The bot returns the current configured announcement. If there is no active announcement, it should say there are no new announcements and suggest menu.

8) Contact admin flow
User sends contact admin, admin, moderator, or support. The bot returns configured admin contact instructions. Optionally, it can create an admincontactrequests record for tracking. In a later release, this can become a more complete ticket-style flow.

9) Group context flow
When the message is from a group, the bot should identify the context from CookMyBots metadata and adjust behavior. It should not pretend to moderate the group if those features are not implemented. It can say: I can help with rules, announcements, and admin contact. Group admin tools are coming soon.

10) Unknown input flow
If the bot cannot understand a message, it should respond with a friendly fallback. The fallback should be short and should guide the user back to the menu. Example behavior: I did not catch that. Type menu to see options, or try rules, announcement, help, or contact admin.

11) Duplicate event flow
Before responding, the bot checks whether the incoming message id was already processed. If yes, it does not send a second reply. This prevents duplicate responses on event retries.

12) Error flow
If a handler fails, the bot logs the error and returns a safe generic message where appropriate: Sorry, I had trouble with that. Please try menu again. The service should avoid exposing internal details to users.

state.title: WhatsApp Community Assistant

aiMessages.content:
Build me a WhatsApp community assistant bot.

The bot should work inside WhatsApp private chats and group chats. It should introduce itself when users say hi, start, or menu. It should have a main menu, help command, settings command, and about command.

The bot should understand simple text commands like:
menu
help
settings
about
rules
announcement
contact admin

When used in a group, the bot should know it is inside a group and explain that group admin features are available soon. It should help users understand the community rules, answer basic questions, and guide users back to the menu when they are confused.

The bot should not depend on slash commands because WhatsApp users may not use them. It should use simple numbered menus and short clear WhatsApp-style replies.

The bot should be friendly, professional, and easy to test.

Built the WhatsApp-only community assistant brain service, removed AI and MongoDB code, added managed webhook handling, private/group menus, safe logging, docs, and local tests.

Files touched (23): .cookmybots/manifest.json, .env.sample, DOCS.md, README.md, package.json, src/bot.js…
Validation: ✅ project passes syntax/basic checks after this edit.
